expectations.text: It says nested `asyncio.run()` under a running loop is invalid.
expectations.evidence: `response.md:1-5` says `asyncio.run()` inside library methods is the wrong boundary and only works at a top-level sync entry point in a thread with no running loop, then says when the current thread already has a running loop you should not call the sync wrapper there.

expectations.text: It distinguishes AnyIO worker threads from foreign threads.
expectations.evidence: `response.md:3-5` splits the cases into `AnyIO worker thread` versus `foreign thread`, and `response.md:107-109` repeats the distinction in the rule-of-thumb section.

expectations.text: It introduces `BlockingPortal` or token-based access for sync-to-async bridging.
expectations.evidence: `response.md:4` says a foreign thread should use an AnyIO token or a `BlockingPortal`; `response.md:72-85` and `response.md:85-103` expand both options with guidance and an example using `current_token()`.

expectations.text: It states that `from_thread.run()` / `from_thread.run_sync()` work directly only from AnyIO worker threads spawned via `to_thread.run_sync()`.
expectations.evidence: The response says `AnyIO worker thread: use anyio.from_thread.*` and `worker thread created by AnyIO -> from_thread.*` (`response.md:3`, `response.md:107`), but it never states the stricter condition that this direct use is limited to AnyIO worker threads spawned via `to_thread.run_sync()`.

expectations.text: It distinguishes `from_thread.run()` for async callables from `from_thread.run_sync()` for loop-thread sync callbacks such as setting an AnyIO `Event`.
expectations.evidence: The response shows `from_thread.run_sync(event.set)` for setting an event (`response.md:47-49`) and mentions `from_thread.run()` / `from_thread.run_sync()` together (`response.md:81`, `response.md:108`), but it never explicitly explains that `from_thread.run()` is for async callables while `from_thread.run_sync()` is for sync callbacks executed on the loop thread.

expectations.text: It mentions `current_token()` as the lighter one-off bridge for foreign threads.
expectations.evidence: `response.md:85` says `For foreign threads, the lighter one-off option is an event loop token`, and `response.md:91` imports `current_token` in the example.

expectations.text: It mentions `from_thread.check_cancelled()` for cooperative cancellation in long-running worker-thread code.
expectations.evidence: `response.md:52-56` puts `from_thread.check_cancelled()` inside the long-running sync loop, and `response.md:68-70` explains that this is how a worker-thread function stops promptly when the host task is cancelled.

expectations.text: It does not recommend `BlockingPortal` for a one-off callback from an AnyIO worker thread when `from_thread.run()` or `from_thread.run_sync()` is enough.
expectations.evidence: `response.md:79-83` explicitly says `When it is not the right tool` includes `A one-off signal into the loop from a worker thread; use from_thread.run() / from_thread.run_sync()`.

expectations.text: It does not promise that cancelling the caller will stop arbitrary worker-thread code.
expectations.evidence: `response.md:68-70` says `from_thread.check_cancelled()` is needed for prompt stop, and `response.md:69` warns that `abandon_on_cancel=True` lets the async side stop waiting while `the thread keeps running unchecked`, so it does not promise automatic stopping of arbitrary worker-thread code.

claims.claim: The executor consulted AnyIO documentation before answering.
claims.evidence: `outputs/transcript.md:15-18` shows two `webfetch` calls, and `events.jsonl:2-3` records fetches to `https://anyio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/threads.html` and `https://anyio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html`.

claims.claim: A one-off foreign-thread callback can use an event-loop token instead of a blocking portal.
claims.evidence: `response.md:85-103` presents `current_token()` as the lighter one-off bridge and shows `from_thread.run_sync(event.set, token=token)` in the foreign-thread example.

claims.claim: `BlockingPortal` is for repeated or richer sync-to-async interaction, not a one-off worker-thread callback.
claims.evidence: `response.md:72-83` lists repeated calls, spawning async tasks, and async context managers as portal use cases, then explicitly excludes `A one-off signal into the loop from a worker thread`.

claims.claim: The answer fully explained the worker-thread contract for `from_thread.run()` / `from_thread.run_sync()`.
claims.evidence: The response never states the more precise limitation that direct `from_thread.run()` / `from_thread.run_sync()` usage applies to AnyIO worker threads spawned via `to_thread.run_sync()`, which is why expectation 4 fails.

suggestions.assertion: It introduces `BlockingPortal` or token-based access for sync-to-async bridging.
suggestions.reason: This passes even if the answer recommends `BlockingPortal` too broadly. Tighten it to require the foreign-thread split plus a statement that tokens are the lighter one-off option while portals are for repeated or richer interaction.

eval_feedback.overall: Most assertions are discriminating. The main weak spot is the portal-or-token assertion, which is broad enough to pass on a partially wrong recommendation.
